source_title: Champions of Trading Profits (FWN Trader Profiles, 1996)
strategy_name: Delta Neutral Hedge: Short Underlying + Long 2x ATM Options

legs.note: 2x ATM calls to achieve delta neutral

structure.dte_range: up to 30 days (Fontanills typical trade duration)

greeks.theta: negative (long options hurt by time decay)
greeks.vega: positive (long options benefit from vol increase)

risk_profile.max_profit: unlimited if underlying moves sharply in either direction (long gamma)
risk_profile.max_loss: premium paid for 2 ATM calls (if underlying stays flat and vol collapses)

entry_conditions: volatility and momentum at extreme levels — 'something is really out of whack' (Fontanills)
contrarian setup: consensus is strongly one-directional
high-volatility market preferred (S&P, bonds, gold, oil, currencies per Fontanills)
existing directional position needs hedge
exit_rules: close within 30 days maximum (Fontanills typical hold)
exit if flat/range-bound — theta decay destroys long option value without movement
adjustment_rules: add or remove call legs to maintain delta neutrality as underlying moves
failure_modes: underlying stays flat — theta destroys long option premium with no directional gain
IV collapse after entry reduces long call value even if underlying moves moderately
over-hedging with too many long calls creates net long gamma position that is expensive

evidence.finding_summary: Fontanills: used options to go delta neutral on futures positions — 'I could hedge myself in both directions and still sleep well at night.' Example given: short wheat + long 2 ATM wheat calls. If wheat falls, profits on short; if wheat rises, call delta accelerates. 'My greatest returns are made when something is really out of whack.' Trade duration up to 30 days maximum.
evidence.notes: Qualitative practitioner interview, 1996. Futures-specific example but concept applies to ETF options. Not directly portable — bot trades ETF options, not futures combos. Concept value: vol-extreme contrarian entry + options to manage directional risk.

model_features: iv_percentile_rank (extreme high = Fontanills entry trigger)
momentum_divergence_flag (vol + price momentum both extreme)
consensus_positioning_z_score (contrarian entry when crowd is one-sided)
delta_neutral_flag

source_title: Champions of Trading Profits (FWN Trader Profiles, 1996)
strategy_name: Volpat Outside Day Reversal: Contrarian Buy After Bearish Outside Day

legs.note: next morning if market opens up after bearish outside day

risk_profile.max_loss: controlled stop below outside day low

entry_conditions: outside day: today's high exceeds prior high AND today's low is below prior low
outside day closes on or near the lows (bearish appearance)
next session opens to the upside (signal confirmation)
underlying must have active volatility — Volpat = volatility + pattern
exit_rules: hold 1-3 days (Gettess typical)
take profit when market gives it — 'if all the market is going to give you is $500, you have to take it'
exit if next-day open confirms direction (gap up = confirmation)
failure_modes: outside day closes on lows AND next session opens lower (no reversal) — do not enter
entering in low-volatility markets — Volpat requires active market movement
holding too long after initial move — 1-3 day hold only

evidence.finding_summary: Gettess (Volpat system, rated top 10 by Futures Truth): 'A big outside day, closing on the lows... most market lore says this is very bearish. What I found is if the market starts up the next day you probably want to buy it.' Volpat = volatility (active market) + pattern recognition (short-term objective patterns). Trade duration 1-3 days.
evidence.notes: Volpat was picked by Futures Truth as one of the top 10 trading systems of all time (as of 1996). Qualitative interview — no specific backtest statistics provided. For bot: applicable as a short-term momentum signal on underlying ETFs. Outside day closing on lows with next-day gap up = bullish CSP / ShortCallSpread entry signal.

model_features: outside_day_flag (high > prev_high AND low < prev_low)
close_location_in_range (close near low of outside day)
next_open_vs_prior_close (gap direction confirmation)
atr_percentile (market must have active volatility — Volpat requirement)
underlying_adv_check

source_title: Champions of Trading Profits (FWN Trader Profiles, 1996)
strategy_name: Commitments of Traders (COT): Commercial Net Position as Regime Signal
entry_conditions: commercials moving from net short to net long: bullish signal for that market
commercials moving from net long to net short: distribution signal, bearish
signal applies across futures markets including S&P 500, T-bonds, currencies
exit_rules: watch for commercial positioning reversal as exit/fade signal
failure_modes: COT data has 3-day reporting lag (released each Friday for positions as of prior Tuesday)
commercials can be wrong on direction in the near term — signal is intermediate-term (weeks to months)
position changes are gradual — accumulation/distribution takes time; not a precise timing tool

evidence.finding_summary: Williams: 'When commercials go from net long to net short — that is usually a good buy signal [on subsequent reversal back]. Commercials don't try to pick tops and bottoms — they accumulate and distribute.' COT data released by CFTC every two weeks (biweekly in 1996, now weekly). Williams: 'When I'm losing in the market I pull back a little and catch my wind.' In bonds: 'Commercials are moving close to being net long — that's pretty bullish.'
evidence.notes: Williams parlayed $10,000 into $1,100,000 in the 1987 Robins World Cup Championship. COT data available free from CFTC (cftc.gov). For bot: S&P 500 futures commercial net positioning is a cross-asset macro regime signal for SPY/QQQ. If commercials are net long S&P futures, regime is more favorable for ShortPutSpread; net short = caution. New signal type not currently in bot.

model_features: cot_commercial_net_position (long - short contracts for key futures: SP500, bonds, gold)
cot_commercial_net_change_4w (direction of change over 4 weeks)
cot_commercial_net_pct_oi (position relative to total open interest)
cot_extreme_flag (commercials at multi-year net long or short extreme)

source_title: Champions of Trading Profits (FWN Trader Profiles, 1996)
strategy_name: Event Trading: Catalyst Reaction Signal (Close Position in Range After Report)
entry_conditions: economic report released (employment, durable goods, PPI, CPI, etc.)
bullish signal: market rallies on news AND closes in upper 20% of the day's range
bearish signal: fundamentally bullish report but market closes in lower 20% of range = sell signal
do NOT enter immediately after report: 'getting in immediately after the number is a 50-50 game'
signal requires close-of-day confirmation — intraday reaction is noise
exit_rules: hold 1-5 days following the signal day
exit at close if position is not working within the first 1-2 days
failure_modes: entering immediately at report release instead of waiting for EOD close confirmation
ignoring close-in-range condition — directional pop without end-of-day confirmation is unreliable
report-driven moves in illiquid markets are noisier

evidence.finding_summary: Warwick (event trading method, published book 1996): 'If the market rallies on the number and by the end of the day it closes in the upper 20% of its range, I take that as a buy signal.' A fundamentally bullish report that results in a close in the lower 20% of range = sell signal. 'I concentrate on how the market reacts to that bit of information.' Trade duration 1-5 days. Most effective reports per Warwick: durable goods orders and employment for bond market.
evidence.notes: Warwick's event trading was developed over 6 years prior to 1996. Close position in range (upper/lower 20%) after economic release is the signal. For bot: applicable as a same-day entry filter — on FOMC/CPI/NFP days, if ETF closes in top/bottom 20% of day range, that signals follow-through direction for next 1-5 days. Could refine macro_blackout to allow entry AFTER the report if reaction is directionally confirmed.

model_features: close_position_in_range (close / (high - low) — upper 20% = 0.80+, lower 20% = 0.20-)
economic_report_day_flag
report_type (employment, CPI, FOMC, durable goods)
intraday_range_vs_atr (large-range day = high information content)
prior_trend_direction (trend acts as baseline — trade in direction of reaction + trend)

source_title: Champions of Trading Profits (FWN Trader Profiles, 1996)
entry_conditions: enter on PeakOscillator signal (momentum indicator, cross-comparable between markets)
Kase: 'My trading techniques are very focused on exit strategies, not entry points'
momentum divergence between price and oscillator: entry on reassertion
exit_rules: primary exit: Dev-Stop (volatility-adjusted stop — stop distance = function of recent volatility, not fixed dollar amount)
partial profit exit on 'danger signals' such as reversal patterns and momentum divergences
Kase: 'I take profits more quickly. I take a lot more winning trades, but the wins are smaller'
take partial profits at first sign of reversal pattern rather than waiting for stop to be hit
adjustment_rules: trail Dev-Stop as position moves in favor
take partial position off on reversal candle or momentum divergence — don't wait for full stop
failure_modes: fixed-dollar stops in volatile markets lead to premature exit — volatility-adjusted stop prevents this
holding too long waiting for maximum profit — momentum divergence is an earlier, more reliable exit signal
applying same stop distance across markets with different volatility profiles

evidence.finding_summary: Kase: Dev-Stop is a 'volatility-adjusted stop technique.' PeakOscillator is a momentum indicator 'that can be cross-compared between markets.' Kase: 'My trading techniques are very focused on exit strategies, not entry points.' 'I take partial profits on danger signals, such as reversal patterns and momentum divergences.' Trade duration 3-10 days. Published in Futures Magazine April/May/June 1996.
evidence.notes: Kase's approach aligns with the bot's Yang-Zhang realized vol estimators — the Dev-Stop concept of scaling stop distance to market volatility is consistent. For bot: stop-loss thresholds on short spreads could be volatility-adjusted (e.g., 2x current ATR rather than fixed credit multiple). Also: partial early close on reversal signal rather than full position management.

model_features: atr_n (ATR over N periods — basis for volatility-adjusted stop)
stop_distance_as_atr_multiple (Dev-Stop concept: stop = f(vol) not fixed $)
momentum_oscillator_divergence_flag (PeakOscillator concept: price new high, oscillator not)
reversal_pattern_flag (candlestick reversal or price pattern — partial exit trigger)

source_title: Champions of Trading Profits (FWN Trader Profiles, 1996)
strategy_name: Momentum Retracement Entry: Trend Direction + Countertrend Pullback

risk_profile.max_loss: defined by stop below retracement low

entry_conditions: must have recently had a strong directional trend (Bierovic: 'real price upthrust in the market')
countertrend retracement occurs — pullback from trend high
trend reasserts itself (price resumes in original trend direction)
minimum liquidity: underlying ADV >= 5,000 contracts, open interest >= 20,000 (Bierovic citing Chuck LeBeau)
exit_rules: exit if market returns to today's high by tomorrow (Bierovic: 'If I get in today, I'd get out tomorrow if the market returns to today's high')
target: doubled the amount risked (Bierovic: 'a good trade is when I doubled what I risk')
2:1 reward/risk minimum; 40% win rate with 2:1 R/R is profitable
adjustment_rules: trail stop intraday (Bierovic watches intraday even on daily-chart setups)
use discretion on exits — take profits on close if 'enough money made'
failure_modes: entering without prior strong trend — 'I won't take a trade unless the market has recently had a strong directional trend'
using in illiquid markets — TA works worse in thin markets
holding too long — most profitable trades are captured quickly; 'markets take profits away nine out of 10 times'

evidence.finding_summary: Bierovic: 'momentum retracement trading method involves knowing the direction and quality of the trend, knowing how to measure countertrend reactions and when the trend has reasserted itself.' Trade duration 2-4 days. Win rate target: 40% minimum with 2:1 reward/risk ratio. Minimum liquidity: ADV >= 5,000 contracts, OI >= 20,000.
evidence.notes: Bierovic manages 'a couple of million dollars' as of 1996. For bot: momentum retracement concept can refine entry timing on ShortPutSpread entries — enter after a pullback in an uptrending ETF rather than chasing momentum. Countertrend dip in an uptrend = better put spread entry than entering at high of trend.

model_features: trend_quality_score (strong vs. weak trend — ADX value)
retracement_depth_pct (how deep the pullback is as % of prior trend leg)
trend_reassertion_flag (price breaks back above prior retracement level)
reward_risk_ratio_at_entry (2:1 minimum)
underlying_adv
open_interest_total

source_title: Champions of Trading Profits (FWN Trader Profiles, 1996)
strategy_name: Cycle + Oscillator Regime: Higher-Timeframe Cycle Sets Trend Bias, Lower-Timeframe Oscillator Sets Entry
entry_conditions: determine higher-timeframe cycle direction (weekly cycle sets daily trend bias per Bressert)
oscillator reading: above 80-90 = probable top; below 10-20-30 = probable bottom
when trend is up: buy dips (oscillator oversold within uptrend)
when trend is down: sell rallies (oscillator overbought within downtrend)
do NOT hold positions going into major scheduled reports — 'that's gambling; the unexpected can happen'
exit_rules: exit on oscillator signal in opposite direction
exit before major scheduled economic reports
failure_modes: using oscillator in isolation without higher-timeframe cycle context — oscillators can stay overbought/oversold in trending markets
holding through macro catalyst events — Bressert: big reports take away the odds

evidence.finding_summary: Bressert: 'Cycles give you the time. Oscillators pick up overbought/oversold energy.' Oscillator > 80-90 = probable top; < 30-20-10 = probable bottom. 'The trend for the daily chart is determined by the weekly cycle.' Key rule: 'when the trend is up, buy dips; when the trend is down, sell rallies.' Does not hold positions going into major economic reports.
evidence.notes: Bressert's rule about avoiding positions through major reports directly supports the bot's macro_blackout module. For bot: higher-timeframe oscillator signal (weekly RSI/stochastic) can act as regime context for daily strategy selection — overbought weekly = avoid ShortCallSpread; oversold weekly = avoid ShortPutSpread.

model_features: weekly_rsi (higher-timeframe momentum for regime context)
daily_stochastic_pct_d (lower-timeframe entry timing)
oscillator_divergence (price makes new high, oscillator does not = exhaustion)
days_to_next_major_report (Bressert blackout rule)
cycle_phase_estimate

source_title: Champions of Trading Profits (FWN Trader Profiles, 1996)
strategy_name: Wyckoff Volume Effort-Result Analysis: Distribution and Accumulation Signals
entry_conditions: accumulation signal: volume rising + price gains shrinking = buying demand being absorbed, not accelerating (caution)
distribution top signal: successive days show increasing volume but decreasing price gain per unit volume
Redmont example: Day 1: 10k shares +1pt, Day 2: 10k +1pt, Day 3: 20k +1pt (double effort, same result), Day 4: 40k +0.5pt, Day 5: 80k flat = distribution top
supply is always present; when demand burns out, prices fall rapidly
exit_rules: exit when effort-result divergence appears: rising volume producing diminishing price gain
failure_modes: volume data quality issues (ETFs can have misleading volume on creation/redemption days)
short-term volume spikes from news or index rebalancing can trigger false signals
Wyckoff requires analysis over multiple days/bars — single-bar volume spike is noise

evidence.finding_summary: Redmont (Wyckoff method): 'The key to analyzing supply and demand is that the demand side burns itself out.' Example: Day 1-2: 10k shares, +1pt each. Day 3: 20k, +1pt. Day 4: 40k, +0.5pt. Day 5: 80k, flat. Rising volume + diminishing price gain = distribution top. 'When the buying is through and satisfied — there is always supply there.' Also: Fibonacci 61.8% and 38.2% retracements are valid because 'markets started connecting' them as they became widely used.
evidence.notes: Redmont trades primarily OEX options (directional, buys calls and puts — not spreads). Wyckoff volume analysis is cross-applicable to ETF volume data. For bot: volume efficiency metric (price gain per unit of additional volume) is a non-standard model feature not in current signal stack. Could supplement the existing volume profile module.

model_features: volume_price_efficiency (price_change / log_volume — diminishing ratio = distribution)
volume_price_divergence_flag (volume making new highs, price gains shrinking)
multi_day_cumulative_effort_result (5-day running ratio of volume to price change)
fibonacci_retracement_proximity (38.2%, 61.8% levels as support/resistance)

source_title: Champions of Trading Profits (FWN Trader Profiles, 1996)
strategy_name: Contrarian Mass Media Sentiment: Cover Story as Top/Bottom Signal

structure.dte_range: 3-5 months (Noble: 'markets are relatively easy to call over a 3-5 month period')

entry_conditions: major mainstream media cover story about a market = contrarian top or bottom signal
media turns very bullish on an asset class = look for top (Noble: NY Times wheat cover = topping day)
media repeatedly negative about an asset class = contrarian buy signal
signal works on 3-5 month time horizon (not intraday)
exit_rules: hold 3-5 months after the contrarian signal
exit when media sentiment reverses
failure_modes: media saturation is rare and hard to systematize — requires manual monitoring
timing is imprecise — cover story signals a zone, not a precise date
works on 3-5 month horizon only — not useful for short-term options entry timing

evidence.finding_summary: Noble: 'The media seem to unfailingly know when there is a top or bottom — it is the best contrarian indicator.' NY Times wheat cover story = topping day in wheat (April 1996). Multiple media stories saying stock market is 'terrible' = buy signal that led to 50% two-year rise. 'Bonds are starting a 6-month rally' triggered by 10-15 negative bond stories in a few weeks.
evidence.notes: 1996 qualitative practitioner view. For bot: FinBERT already scores financial news sentiment. Mass media saturation could be approximated by: unusually high FinBERT article volume + extreme sentiment reading for a given ETF sector over rolling 14-day window. Not a precise entry signal but a regime filter — avoid counter-trend entries when sentiment is extreme.

model_features: finbert_sentiment_14d_ma (rolling average for extreme readings)
news_volume_spike_flag (article count per ticker in past 14d vs 90d baseline)
finbert_sentiment_z_score (standard deviations from rolling mean — extreme = contrarian signal)
contrarian_media_flag (sentiment z-score beyond ±2 for 5+ consecutive days)

source_title: Champions of Trading Profits (FWN Trader Profiles, 1996)
strategy_name: Stowell Cups and Caps: 3-Bar Reversal Pattern

risk_profile.max_profit: open (at least 2 profitable closes per Stowell)
risk_profile.max_loss: stop set to extreme of the 3-bar formation

entry_conditions: CUP (bearish reversal after rally): 3-bar pattern where middle bar has LOWER close/low than bars 1 and 3
cup trigger: daily close below all 3 lows of the cup formation = SELL signal
CAP (bullish reversal after decline): opposite of cup — middle bar has HIGHER close/high
cap trigger: daily close above all 3 highs of the cap formation = BUY signal
prior move must exist: cup requires prior rally; cap requires prior decline
exit_rules: look for two profitable closes then exit
stop at extreme of the 3-bar formation (cup: stop = highest high of the 3 bars; cap: stop = lowest low)
remove stop in front of economic reports to avoid stop-running on news spikes
adjustment_rules: trail stop after first profitable close
failure_modes: pattern without prior trend — cups after sideways consolidation, not after rallies, are less reliable
stop too tight relative to market volatility — Stowell: remove stop during economic reports
holding through economic reports — 'inviting the market to pick your stop off and you get huge slippage'

evidence.finding_summary: Stowell: cup = 3-bar pattern where middle bar has lower close/low, bars 1 and 3 have higher closes/lows. Trigger: close below all 3 lows. Cap = opposite. Developed in 1984. Grew $5,000 to $100,000 in just over 2 years (1985-1987) using cups and caps only. Applied to Treasury bond futures. Holds for 'two profitable closes.' Stop = extreme of the 3-bar formation.
evidence.notes: Stowell applied primarily to Treasury bond futures. For bot: 3-bar reversal pattern is applicable to ETF daily price data. Cup pattern = underlying has rallied and may be reversing = watch for ShortCallSpread entry opportunity. Cap pattern = underlying has fallen and may be reversing = watch for ShortPutSpread entry opportunity.

model_features: cup_pattern_flag (3-bar: bar1.low < bar2.low > bar3.low AND close < min(bar1.low, bar2.low, bar3.low))
cap_pattern_flag (3-bar: bar1.high > bar2.high < bar3.high AND close > max(bar1.high, bar2.high, bar3.high))
prior_trend_bars (cups require prior rally; caps require prior decline)
formation_extreme (stop reference: max high for cup, min low for cap)
days_to_next_economic_report (remove stop near reports per Stowell)

source_title: Champions of Trading Profits (FWN Trader Profiles, 1996)
strategy_name: Candlestick Real Body Analysis: Open-Close Relationship as Sentiment Signal
entry_conditions: white candle (empty body): close above open = bulls won the day's battle
black candle (filled body): close below open = bears won the day's battle
real body size: large real body = decisive sentiment; small real body (doji) = indecision
shadows: long upper shadow = selling pressure rejected the rally; long lower shadow = buying absorbed the dip
combine with Western indicators (moving averages, stochastics, trendlines) for confirmation
exit_rules: bearish reversal candlestick pattern (e.g., large black candle after white candles) = partial or full exit trigger
doji after trend run = indecision, consider tightening stop
failure_modes: candlestick patterns require context — a bearish candle in a strong uptrend is noise, not a reversal signal
in highly liquid ETFs with program trading, candle patterns may be less reliable than in individual stocks
Wagner: moved from day-trading to position trading as large institutional players changed intraday dynamics

evidence.finding_summary: Wagner: candlesticks are 'a mathematical expression of psychological market sentiment.' Key distinction: Western TA focuses on close-to-close; candlesticks show open-to-close relationship (real body). White candle = close above open; black candle = close below open. After incorporating candlesticks, 'my trading vastly improved. I started making money and my clients started making money.' Combine with Western TA tools (MA, stochastics, trendlines). 'The most profitable way to trade is with the trend.'
evidence.notes: For bot: open-to-close relationship (real body direction and size) is an additional price action feature. Real body as % of total range provides a normalized sentiment signal distinct from simple close-to-close return. Implementable as daily feature: real_body_pct = abs(open - close) / (high - low).

model_features: real_body_pct (abs(open-close) / (high-low) — large = decisive; small = indecision)
candle_direction (close > open = bullish body; close < open = bearish body)
upper_shadow_pct ((high - max(open, close)) / (high - low) — large = selling pressure at highs)
lower_shadow_pct ((min(open, close) - low) / (high - low) — large = buying support at lows)
doji_flag (real_body_pct < 0.1 — indecision candle)

source_title: Champions of Trading Profits (FWN Trader Profiles, 1996)
strategy_name: Market Selection Pre-Filter: Volatility and Liquidity Minimum Thresholds
entry_conditions: Angell: both volatility AND liquidity must be present — one without the other is insufficient
Gettess/Bierovic/Reynolds/Wagner: avoid illiquid markets (pork bellies, lumber, OJ, coffee, cotton)
Bierovic (citing LeBeau): minimum ADV >= 5,000 contracts, open interest >= 20,000
Gettess: 'Volpat' requires an active market — if market isn't moving, you can't make money
multiple traders: large liquid markets are less subject to manipulation, better TA signal quality
exit_rules: exit any market where volatility has 'dried up' — Angell: gold market died in 1980 when both vol and liquidity declined
failure_modes: entering illiquid markets: 'a big fish can move a little pond' — Bierovic
entering low-volatility markets: insufficient premium / movement to profit
staying in a market after volatility drops (Angell: 'markets die on you')

evidence.finding_summary: Multiple traders across all 18 profiles independently emphasize: trade only liquid, volatile markets. Angell: 'Liquidity and volatility are the two things you have to have.' Bierovic (LeBeau threshold): ADV >= 5,000, OI >= 20,000. Reynolds: 'The bigger the market, the better your fills are and the better technical analysis works.' Gettess: markets must be actively moving for Volpat to work. Wagner: avoids coffee and cotton due to liquidity/slippage.
evidence.notes: Cross-validated by 7+ independent traders in the same interview set. Minimum liquidity thresholds are for futures contracts; for ETF options, equivalent thresholds are already in bot (underlying ADV >= 500k, option OI >= 100/500). For bot: consider adding a volatility floor to market selection — minimum 20-day realized vol threshold before entry, beyond just IV rank.

model_features: underlying_adv_check (min threshold varies: futures ADV >= 5,000; ETF equiv >= 500k shares)
option_open_interest_check (bot already has this: >= 100, prefer 500+)
realized_vol_20d_floor (minimum realized vol — avoid entering when market is truly dead)
bid_ask_spread_pct (options spread as % of mid — liquidity proxy)
volume_regime_flag (vol + liquidity both above threshold = tradeable)
